title: Is it a bad sign that my mom's pulmonologist bumped her appointment on Thursday, to tomorrow? Two weeks ago, he saw a spot on her MRI, and Saturday she had a PET scan with nuclear tracers and sugar injected. Also, why does it seem like people who stop smoking get cancer right after they quit?
body:
I assume the bumped appointment is a bad sign. I'm having some horrible guilt right now as well. 

In the past, my family has known people who've died from lung cancer. Most of them were diagnosed within a few months of smoking cessation. A little over a year ago, my 70 yo mom fell down some stairs. I came to stay with her while she healed, and two weeks later... she had a heart attack. She's had COPD (emphysema & chronic bronchitis) for quite a while now... but after the heart attack, she's been on constant oxygen. I've moved in permanently with my family to take care of her. 

This past summer, she developed pneumonia. She spent a week in the hospital, and during a bad coughing fit, suffered another heart attack. She came home, and first thing she did was smoke a cigarette. She had been smoking the whole time. 

Being her caregiver (and a smoker as well) I began to really push for us both to quit. She didn't want too, so I basically guilted her into it. Also used some manipulating threats, essentially. It's truly how I was feeling tho... but in a nutshell, I said that I'm feeling like we're doing everything for her for nothing. I pretty much said, why should I take you to all these doctor appointments, fill your prescriptions, dish out your pills and nebulizer treatments... hold you when you're scared and can't breathe, cook and clean... if you're going to undo it all by smoking? 

Well... her main answer, was "because I've had friends who were fine smoking, but got cancer when they quit". I guess I wore her down enough, and in September, she finally quit. I switched to vaping, and have decreased the nicotine content dramatically. Soon I will stop that too, cause I feel like a giant hypocrite. 

By the end of November, she actually started feeling better! She was so happy! She said things like "I should've quit years ago". But she consistently still joked "watch, I'm going to get lung cancer because I quit tho". Around Christmas, she was having some chest pain. We went to the ER and she had a chest x-ray, blood work, and an EKG. Amazingly, her pneumonia was finally cleared, and her EF (ejection fraction) was up to 55 from 35! Which means her heart was stronger. The chest pain was likely from the Cardiac Rehab exercises. She said that was the best Christmas present she's ever had!  

Then... about 2 weeks ago, she had a follow up with her pulmonologist. A "regular" 4 month follow up, and her yearly MRI of her lungs. Her pulmonologist is on the verge of retirement... and he's been switching up her appointments. Somehow, it worked out that she had her MRI 2 hours before her appointment. Which was just a coincidence. We sat with him, and he first showed us the x-ray from December. He pointed out some scarring in her bottom left right lung from the pneumonia. I then asked if he had the results from the MRI yet. He was surprised when we said she just had one hours before. He pulled it up, and reviewed it in front of us. He went through the pictures or whatever... and showed us the scars from the pneumonia. He was using a pen to show "there's some scarring there, some scarring there" and then he pointed to a tiny spot and said "and probably some scaring there". I could see the wheels turning in his head though, at that moment. He then said "well, wait" and switched to a different view where he could go throu the individual "slices". He went back and forth a few times... and said "I'd like you to get a PET scan". He said that the radiologist hasn't written the report yet, but I'd like a better look at that spot. 

He then went into an oncology lesson. Saying, that he thinks the oncology department here treats tumors without a biopsy. If it's cancerous, they would likely blast it with radiation... but she probably wouldn't need chemotherapy. My mom made a joking comment "maybe it's good you saw this now". The doc said "why, you might have cancer". She said "well if you're just seeing it, maybe we caught it early". In which he said "the fact that it wasn't there 3 weeks ago, isn't good at all". 

A few days later, the nurse called with a PET scan appointment for this past Saturday. She had to fast, and skip her diabetes meds. I guess they inject some sort of radioactive glucose or something? Which should "light up a tumor like a Christmas tree"? This doctor is only working Tuesday to Thursday because he's trying to retire. She had an appointment set up for Thursday, but the office called at 8 o'clock this morning to change her appointment to Tuesday. We're both freaking out about it. Even my mom commented "if it was nothing, they would've scheduled for a later date". I tend to agree. Although, you never know with this doctor.

Of course, my mom had to do the "told you so" dance. She said that she never should've quit smoking. I tried to look it up, and of course there's nothing saying that smoking prevents cancer lol. But, it really seems like anyone we've known has been diagnosed AFTER they quit. Also, we've noticed a trend where once someone is cut open to remove a tumor... it seems to help it spread. My mom said it's almost like the oxygen getting to the cancer is like throwing gas on a fire. I know these things are unconfirmed... but it does seem weird that's what's happened with the people we know. 

Any thoughts or opinions are welcome. I could use some support and information as well. Not just medical texts... but real life experience too. I'm almost tempted to have her start smoking again! Not really... but it's crossed my mind. I almost wonder if the tar and inflammation from smoking, as well as the constriction  of blood vessels... were keeping any potential cancer on check. I'm almost thinking that since her lungs cleared, and blood flow/oxygen returned to parts... the cancer grew? Like watering a seed?
